user_goals_summary: This user writes immersive, first-person travel and food essays that aim to make the reader feel physically present in a specific place and moment. They prioritize original sensory language over polished food-writing conventions, and want personal interiority woven into observation so the experience carries emotional stakes, not just atmosphere.

rubric.name: Multi-Sensory Immersion
rubric.description: Scenes should feel inhabited, not just observed. Non-visual senses — temperature, sound, smell, texture — are as important as visual detail. The reader should feel like they're standing in the room, not looking at a photograph.

dimensions.label: At least two non-visual senses (sound, smell, temperature, touch/texture) are concretely rendered in key scenes
The physical environment is felt on the body — temperature of air, warmth of surfaces, closeness of space — not just seen
Includes small, true-feeling physical actions (shifting weight, putting something down, pausing mid-gesture) that ground the narrator in the scene
Sounds are named precisely rather than gestured at — a specific rhythm, a particular quality of noise, not just 'it was noisy' or 'it was quiet'

rubric.name: Original, Precise Language
rubric.description: Avoids food and travel writing clichés ('rich,' 'delicious,' 'hidden gem'). Key descriptions use language specific enough to make the reader pause and reread. Flavor and experience are rendered through unexpected comparisons and precise physical observations, not elevated-sounding adjectives.

dimensions.label: Flavor and experience descriptions avoid stock food-writing language (rich, delicious, mouthwatering, authentic, etc.)
Contains at least one description or image striking enough to make the reader stop and reread the sentence
Key sensory moments use unexpected comparisons or precise physical observations rather than conventional praise
Describes what something did to the narrator rather than declaring how good it was

rubric.name: Place-Experience Integration
rubric.description: The setting and the central experience (a meal, a discovery) are woven together throughout — the room doesn't disappear when the food arrives. Environment, action, and sensation happen simultaneously, so the experience feels inseparable from the specific place it occurred.

dimensions.label: The physical environment remains present during the central experience rather than dropping away
Setting details and experience details are interleaved within paragraphs rather than separated into distinct description blocks
The qualities of the place (its size, noise level, steadiness, indifference) actively inflect how the experience feels
Other people in the space are briefly but specifically felt — sounds, movements, presence — grounding the scene in shared reality

rubric.name: Personal Interiority
rubric.description: First-person observation includes the narrator's inner life — a memory, an emotional context, a piece of personal history — that gives the experience weight beyond the moment. Not a digression, but a brief crack in the window that earns the intensity of what follows.

dimensions.label: The reader understands why this experience mattered to the narrator specifically, not just that it was good
At least one moment where the narrator's history, mood, or trip context briefly surfaces to frame the present
Personal threads are woven in with restraint — a sentence or two at most — rather than extended backstory or reflection
The personal moment functions as a hook that deepens what comes after it, not as a standalone aside

rubric.name: Pacing and Lingering
rubric.description: The writing slows down and gives space to the moments that mattered most. Key experiences are lingered in rather than summarized or rushed past. Transitions feel earned rather than abrupt.

dimensions.label: The most important moment in the piece receives noticeably more sentence-level attention than surrounding material
The central experience is not compressed into a quick summary or single paragraph when it deserves to breathe
The prose rhythm shifts — shorter sentences, pauses, a beat of stillness — to signal when something matters

rubric.name: Conversational Warmth and Voice
rubric.description: The tone is warm, intimate, and conversational — like being told a story by a friend, not reading a polished magazine feature. The reader feels pulled into the experience alongside the narrator rather than addressed or informed.

dimensions.label: Sentences read with a natural spoken cadence — dashes, fragments, asides — rather than formal prose structure
The reader is positioned as a companion in the experience rather than an audience being informed about it
Emotional moments are warm but earned — not oversold with exclamation or declarative sentiment
Closing beats land with quiet weight rather than grand summary statements or tidy moral conclusions

coaching_notes: This user's revision instinct is almost always to push from general to specific — if they say something 'flattens out,' they mean it's reached for conventional beauty instead of physical truth. They value restraint alongside specificity: one well-placed personal thread, not three; enough sensory detail to inhabit a scene, not so much it crowds the prose. They respond extremely well to being pushed back on when they're solving a problem that's already been solved — they respect the assistant holding the structural picture and redirecting rather than just complying.

rubric.name: Place-Experience Integration
rubric.description: The setting and the central experience are woven together throughout — the room doesn't disappear when the food arrives or the moment deepens. Environment, action, and sensation happen simultaneously, so the experience feels inseparable from the specific place it occurred.

dimensions.label: The physical environment remains present during the central experience rather than dropping away
Setting details and experience details are interleaved within paragraphs rather than separated into distinct description blocks
The qualities of the place (its size, noise level, steadiness, indifference) actively inflect how the experience feels
Other people in the space are briefly but specifically felt — sounds, movements, presence — grounding the scene in shared reality
The setting is depicted as having its own life and routines independent of the narrator — not staged or oriented toward the visitor

rubric.name: Pacing and Lingering
rubric.description: The writing slows down and gives space to the moments that mattered most. Whether the piece centers on a single focal experience or an accumulative mood, the prose makes room for the reader to dwell where it counts rather than rushing through at a uniform speed.

dimensions.label: The most important moment or feeling in the piece receives noticeably more sentence-level attention than surrounding material
The central experience — whether a single moment or an accumulative feeling — is not compressed into a quick summary when it deserves to breathe
The prose rhythm shifts — shorter sentences, pauses, a beat of stillness — to signal when something matters

rubric.name: Conversational Warmth and Voice
rubric.description: The tone is warm, intimate, and conversational — like being told a story by a friend, not reading a polished magazine feature. Humor and asides emerge organically from the situation rather than being inserted to signal casual charm.

dimensions.label: Sentences read with a natural spoken cadence — dashes, fragments, asides — rather than formal prose structure
The reader is positioned as a companion in the experience rather than an audience being informed about it
Emotional moments are warm but earned — not oversold with exclamation or declarative sentiment
Closing beats land with quiet weight rather than grand summary statements or tidy moral conclusions
Self-aware or humorous asides feel like they emerged naturally from the situation — not constructed to signal the narrator doesn't take themselves too seriously
Lines that sound emotionally meaningful but don't say anything concrete ('exactly where I was supposed to be,' 'something indescribable') are treated as failures of voice, not poetic restraint

This user trusts restraint over volume — one precise image outperforms three adequate ones, and one sentence of personal context outperforms a paragraph of backstory.

Real experiences have weird human edges, and the writing should too.
